original_abstract: We present NeuroVault (neurovault.org), an open-source, web-based repository designed for the storage, sharing, and interactive visualization of statistical maps of the human brain. While traditional publications often reduce complex 3D data to static 2D figures and limited tables of peak coordinates, NeuroVault allows researchers to upload and explore full unthresholded volumes. The platform utilizes modern web technologies to provide browser-based 2D and 3D visualization without the need for specialized software. By integrating with the Neurosynth database, NeuroVault enables real-time "cognitive decoding" to help researchers systematically interpret their findings. Furthermore, a public REST API ensures the data is programmatically accessible, fostering interoperability with other neuroinformatics tools. By facilitating the transition from coordinate-based to image-based meta-analysis, NeuroVault provides a critical resource for improving the transparency, longevity, and cumulative power of neuroimaging research.
humanized_abstract: Abstract Background There are only a few openly accessible databases that store neuroimaging statistical maps. When studies are published the data are often greatly diminished: a few hundred coordinates are printed in supplemental tables and the three-dimensional aspects of the findings are relegated to two-dimensional figures. Much of this information is lost to future studies that would like to consider similar effects in prior literature. Results We present a web application, NeuroVault (neurovault.org), that allows researchers to store, search, view and overlap statistical maps. A wealth of neuroimaging data can be presented in a fully unthresholded three-dimensional form and analyzed using online tools built directly into the web application. NeuroVault connects to the Neurosynth database (neurosynth.org) to enable cognitive decoding of new data (i.e. relating a voxel-wise statistical map back into a cognitive domain) and uses a public REST API so that data in the database may be downloaded, processed and re-uploaded by other neuroinformatics tools. Conclusions NeuroVault allows for the enhanced archiving and reuse of neuroimaging results that are increasingly being reported at the level of whole volumes (i.e. image-based results). We hope that the database will promote increased transparency, data archival and ultimately the cumulative progress of the field.
